text: The more I think about Zhao the less sense his trajectory makes. He starts out as a commander, then he gets promoted to admiral about a third of the way through Season 1. From what I can tell, the three seasons' timeline is about a year or so, which means the entirety of Season 1 must have taken a few months at best. During this very short time as admiral, he admittedly nearly captures the Avatar, but this ends in disaster when one single masked warrior manages to break the Avatar out of imprisonment, despite the presence of Zhao and all his soldiers. Not to mention the fact that Zhao gets his ass beaten in a duel with a teenager, only to disgracefully try to attack him from behind when the fight is over. Then he also leads an expedition into the wilderness to hunt down a famous deserter, but all it does is lead to him destroying his own boats and letting the deserter, the Avatar, and dozens of others evade capture. With all those defeats and disgraces in mind, how exactly did Zhao get appointed supreme command over the invasion of the North? I'm not talking about why the writers put him there; he's the secondary villain and so he's gotta be front and centre when it comes to the big showdown in the season finale. I get that part. But what's the in-universe explanation? He's only newly promoted to admiralty. Wouldn't there have been several other admirals with seniority who'd have jumped at the chance to lead an invasion of the North Pole? This is the biggest military operation since the Siege of Ba Sing Se. How did Zhao get dibs to take command? I get that people fail upward all the time, but I don't think we're ever given a moment where Zhao's positive traits are shown, or any kind of indication that he's well-liked or has top connections, or whatever. He's just a big blustery guy with severe anger issues and a lack of common sense, a grown man who got knocked down by a teenager and who got manipulated by a child... and both those things happened in front of witnesses who would surely tell other people about them.
label: analysis

text: Azula was not weaker in the comet fight, not in the way you were implying. Yes, Azula was not thinking as clearly or strategically, instead, her power was ramped up 10 fold. Contrary to popular belief, if you have enough power *strategy doesn't fucking matter.* Look at Iroh. For 100 years the Fire Nation tried to break through the walls of Ba Sing Se, and Iroh barely managed to breech the outermost wall. With the comet, he blew the fucker clean open, no strategy or tactics needed. Azula was every bit as deadly, even more so, during the comet as she was without the comet and with her senses about her. Azula's loss of sanity in that fight made her even more dangerous because she is willing to do things she never would normally. Ask any person with real combat experience, an unpredictable person is way more dangerous than otherwise. It's why you can have highly trained fighters be wary of fighting someone with no training, because the person with no training is going to do things and risk things a train fighter never would, and that can't be easily predicted. Regardless, in a fair fight, Azula loses to Katara every time. Katara fundamentally counters Azula on a meta-combatant level. *How* Katara fights is anathema to how Azula fights. Azula actually fared better against Katara under the comet precisely because Azula was fighting in a way she normally doesn't, relying more on brute force and overwhelming power. That's how Zuko fights, and that's why Zuko is better at fighting Katara than Azula is. Katara also has some of the highest battlefield awareness and intelligence of anyone in the series. She is constantly aware of her surroundings and whats going on. Only Toph beats her in that regard. But Katara also has insane control and precision. Look at when Mai and Katara first met in Omashu. Mai threw daggers at Katara and Katara used a small water whip to break floorboards and launch them into the path of the daggers. That's a fucking incredible amount of control and understanding of her powers that Azula has never displayed.
label: analysis

text: I would agree that Aang does have lowkey better earthbending feats than waterbending feats, but I’m not sure if that was actually the writer’s intention. In Sozin’s Comet Part 1, Zuko says that Aang still hasn’t fully mastered firebending, and Toph comments that his earthbending could still use some work too, but Katara doesn’t say anything about his waterbending being inadequate. Waterbending was also the easiest bending style for Aang to pick up. He started waterbending pretty much immediately when Katara started training him in “The Waterbending Scroll,” and even outperformed Katara in waterbending in that episode. Pakku even acknowledged that Aang is a waterbending prodigy, but he didn’t improve as much as Katara did under Pakku’s training because Katara was taking her training seriously while Aang was goofing off. Aang’s personality is also just better suited for waterbending than it is for earthbending. This makes me believe that Aang’s proficiency in the elements is supposed to be ordered like this by the finale: air (obviously) > water > earth > fire (mainly because he had little time to master fire bending before the finale). I feel like Aang just didn’t get enough chances to show off his waterbending skills because the writers didn’t want him to step on Katara’s feet too much, and Aang was put in more scenarios where Toph wasn’t around to earthbend (like in Crossroads of Destiny) than he was scenarios where Katara wasn’t around to waterbend
label: analysis

text: Korra haters are some of the most unbearable people in the Avatar fandom. We just got a brief summary of the next Avatar series, and they’re already jumping to conclusions with, “It was her fault,” “She’s a horrible person,” “F@$k her.” They refuse to consider context—something I always stand by. And of course, the replies are full of people using this as an excuse to be blatantly bigoted, throwing around terms like “woke,” “lesbo man-haters,” “women ☕️,” and “DEI Avatar.” One look at the account behind this tweet, and it’s clear they’re a terrible person—hell, they’re even a lol!con 🤮. The replies are just as bad, packed with accounts spamming “DEI Avatar” and “This is what happens when you put a gay Avatar in charge,” alongside other disgusting takes. I’m so sick of these people. Hating Korra is their entire personality, and they act like Korra fans never acknowledge her flaws—which is just NOT true LOL. A lot of us love her because she’s flawed and more relatable. The difference is, we actually use critical thinking and understand the context—Nickelodeon kept telling the creators that the series would end after Seasons 1 and 2, which messed up the story. And let’s be real, it’s hard to relate to an Avatar who was raised by Buddhist monks, known for their emotional intelligence and stability. I love Aang, but I don’t see myself in him much because I never had that privilege. Unless you live in one of the few countries where Buddhism is the predominant religion, there’s not much to relate to.

text: Suki has no personality and her only purpose is being Sokka's girlfriend. Suki's initial character hinged on her being a warrior woman, in a very overt "girls can fight too!" capacity, which was fine for an early episode. She did her bit and Sokka learned his lesson. Then the show found its stride, and by the time Suki came back there was already a much bigger cast of fighting girls like Katara, Toph, Azula, even other non-benders like Mai and Ty-Lee. So, lacking her original niche, she was left with her aesthetic (which is admittedly killer) and...being Sokka's love interest. And the writers really didn't do anything with her beyond that. She has absolutely no flaws, no growth, we don't even know a single detail about her life other than being a Kyoshi warrior. Why did she join? What was her home life like? What conflicts did she face to be where she was? Mai and Ty-Lee were given more depth in five minutes on Ember Island than is ever given to Suki. Even the comics don't take any opportunities to expand on her as a character, she's just Sokka's warrior girlfriend. I don't hate her, it's just jarring to have a member of the Gaang, who was one of the key players in the finale, feel so empty as a person.

text: When I watched TLoK around the time it first came out, I couldn’t understand why the Equalist movement was even a thing. There were bender gangs and the police were incompetent, but there were various cases of non-benders leading incredibly successful lives (for better or worse) in both series. The wealthiest man in Republic City was a non-bender while the supposed “superior class” consisting of people like Mako and Bolin were struggling to make ends meet. Then you had people like Tenzin and Lin who actively fought for equality as well. It seemed like a non-existent problem even into Book 2 where Varrick, another non-bender, had so much power that he was one of the leading instigators of the Water Tribe Civil War. Then, I watched Book 3… Zaofu is a flagship authoritarian ethnostate that promotes genetic determinism in the subtlest way possible. As a matter of fact, Zaofu literally checks every single box for the tenants of fascism as a whole. Everything in Zaofu is controlled by a single, elite bending family with no elections and no opposition. Suyin has a cult of personality centered around herself. The core philosophy of the city’s population is perfection and progress through bending. It may not seem like it, but militarism is baked into Zaofu’s culture and bleeds into civilian life. THE PEOPLE OF THE CITY LITERALLY WORK TOGETHER TO ENCLOSE THE PLACE IN A SERIES OF GIANT METAL DOMES FOR CRYING OUT LOUD! A non-bender has no place in this society except as a second-class citizen at best. This is a place where non-benders aren’t just an oppressed class, they quite literally CAN’T exist. Sure, a non-bender could theoretically live in Zaofu, but what could they possibly do there? The city was designed with metalbenders at the forefront. Even firebenders and waterbenders would struggle to compete with the earthbenders for work. Opal and the Baatars are the notable exceptions as they’re the matriarch’s family, but clearly they’re not the rule.
label: analysis

text: One thought that occurs to me about Zuko's Agni Kai with his father, is the incident that got him into this mess. He objects to a generals plan to send an inexperienced division against an Earth Kingdom battalion. But the thing about a division is that it is comprised of many battalions. The general is planning to send it against a much *smaller* force. He is giving them the easiest first battle he can. So either writers did not know this, or this is a case in which Zuko hears the generals tasteless joke about "fresh meat", jumps to the wrong conclusion, and shouts out an intervention that makes him look an idiot to everyone in the room. On the one hand, you can see why this would make Ozai even more furious with Zuko. On the other, it all being a well-meaning *mistake* on Zuko's part, followed by his having to decide between disobeying his father (treason) and attempting to strike him (also treason) makes the whole thing even more tragic.
label: analysis

text: The Roku novels seem to have polarizing opinions from fanbase, with many feeling like they didn't do too much to add to the lore or the overall worldbuilding of the universe. But, I've gotta say Roku's personal journey and growth and how they tied it to him learning the elements has to be my favorite way they've depicted an Avatar in training. With him going from the start of first book with him being very abrasive, impatient, hotheaded noble, especially with Gyatso, to being a very tranquil, open minded, very rational individual at the end of the first book and throughout the second book and this behavioral change is tied to when he starts airbending. We see a bit more of this in the second novel as well. He had a twin that died from drowning, since then Roku had started associating water with death, which severely hindered his ability to waterbend when he first started training, and as we are told a few times throughout the story that Roku had insecurities about his slow progression as far as learning the elements went. Low and behold at the end of the second novel, after learning a bit of waterbending, accepts his slow progression and even decides to spend time in the North just learning and living in their culture instead of immediately starting his waterbending training. All in all what I'm trying to say is I loved how they directly tie his elemental training to his overall mentality as he grows and matures and how these different mindsets help him achieve that maturity.

text: How Korra was raised is really striking. She grew up in a fortified compound in the middle of nowhere, forbidden from leaving and unable to visit anyone outside it, except perhaps on a handful of days a year. Her parents lived far away from her, and I think she was only able to spend time with other kids a handful of times a year. The White Lotus and her parents lied to her, telling her that this isolation was the wish of her past life, when really it was their decision. She was ordered around by the White Lotus, taught to obey them, and chided whenever she didn't. Tenzin scolds her for violating the "orders of the White Lotus," even though she is 17 and even though her parents were completely fine with her leaving. Who does the White Lotus think they are, to order around a nearly adult Avatar like that? And finally and perhaps most importantly, Korra was relentlessly trained to be Avatar while being denied to the opportunity to develop any sort of identity outside of that. I know that it was all justified as "protecting her," but the reality is that the White Lotus treated her more as a weapon than a person. They denied her anything even remotely resembling a normal childhood, in complete rejection of tradition. I say this all because I think two of the most prominent traits of Korra as a character are that she struggles to develop any sort of sense of who she is other than being the Avatar and that she's isolated, naive, and cut off from the broader world and has never developed the skills to cope with it. Both of these can be laid at the feet of how the White Lotus raised her. One interesting thing about TLoK is that, although so much of show is about Korra struggling to overcome the ways her childhood limited her and stunted her, none of the people who are responsible for hurting Korra are ever really called out for what they did.
label: analysis

text: I wish ATLA would have explored the ambiguity of Long Feng/Kuei more. Long Feng was almost certainly the one who repelled Iroh's siege. Ba Sing Se spent a century unconquered and that had *nothing* to do with the Earth King, Long Feng and his predecessors spent 100 years successfully protecting the city. The Earth King takes control and the city falls in days because he (mostly) blindly trusted whoever showed up. Sure, Sokka vouched for them but...any competent ruler would have vetted them still. Long Feng would have had Azula in a jail cell in an hour. I wish the show would have grappled with this a bit more. Azula would have been *far* less likely to succeed if Long Feng were still in actual power and not sitting in a jail cell. Ba Sing Se fell because the Gaang chose incompetent good over competent evil. But the show never really talks about that and I feel like it's a giant missed opportunity. I'm not even saying the characters made the wrong choice, but they should maybe question that choice a bit
label: analysis

text: Zuko's scar represents many things, his missing honor, his shame, his exile, later down the line, his dual linage and a whole bunch of other things that can be said about what it represents. It is also used for visual storytelling where a choice or something about Zuko is shown through how the camera positions Zuko and the entire conversation around him. Image 1: Iroh questions if Zuko remembers the last time he dueled a master with Zuko responding in kind that "I will never forget" with the camera showing only the scarred side when he says that. Now, we know now it was Ozai who did it but in the context of the episode placement and when it released, it already gives us an origin for how Zuko got the scar and what it means to him in the conversation before with Zhao, way before we get to "The Storm" where we see what triggered his banishment and quest for the Avatar and it does all that with a simple sentence and utilizing the camera. It helps foreshadow what’s to come later down the line by giving us the broad answers (how he got the scar (Agni Kai from a master) and what it means (disappointment, failure and dishonor)) before we get into the specifics of how it happened. Image 2: Azula has just tracked down Zuko and Iroh to try and bring them back into the Fire Nation as prisoners welcomed back royals by spinning a story about how Ozai fears a plot to overthrow him so he can only rely on family. Just before Azula says that Ozai would welcome him back and that he regrets banishing him (he doesn’t), the camera spins around so that Azula covers up his scarred side, she covers up the shame, dishonor and disappointment that the scar represents to Zuko before telling us what her great news brings.
label: analysis

text: There is one thing I hate about Book 4's portrayal of Korra's trauma. And it's how it magically goes away after just one conversation with Zaheer. text: Hot take: Korra has more depth and character development than Aang. Whether you like Korra or hate her, she changes dramatically through her journey. Aang changes through his too, but compared to Korra he pretty much stays the same, or stays true to himself. I think this is also connected to how both shows portray power. Aang begins as an airbender master, but is shocked to learn he should fullfill a role with even more power that is being the Avatar. His journey is about growing stronger and gaining power in order to be the good person with a lot of power that defeats the bad person with a lot of power. Korra on the other hand starts her journey with having trained her whole life to be strong and having power so she can follow in Aangs footsteps, not only did having power help him with his probably greatest achievment, aka dethroning Ozai, she was also raised under the white lotus that trained her to be strong and though. Since Korras story focuses way more on the multiple conflicts with power and what it means to use it beneficially, especially in a time of peace, it deconstructs way more about her and her character than with Aang and makes her more layered and gives her more depth. I am NOT saying Aang is badly written or a horrible character. I am just saying that Korra is, at least in my opinion, better written wich has a lot to do with how both shows are dealing with power and the use of it.
label: analysis

text: Korra's regular Book 1 and Book 4 outfits are more or less the same so I don't think there's as much intentional symbolism behind them, however I do think the evolution between her Book 1 and Book 4 formal attire holds much more weight to her development and identity as the Avatar. The Book 1 formal outfit we see her wear looks fine enough, but something about it has always struck me as off and I couldn't quite place my finger on it. The more I thought about it though, the more I realized that she simply just looks kind of... well, awkward in it. The outfit on its own isn't bad and I'm not even saying that Korra looks bad in it, but every time I see it, it almost gives me the vibe you get while seeing a teenager be forced to wear church clothes they grew out of a couple years ago but didn't have anything else to wear. It doesn't feel like proper attire for someone just about to enter adulthood, and this is only accentuated seeing her stand beside her team (see second pic). Asami, Mako, and Bolin look like actual adults and seem more comfortable in their appearances, with much fancier and more elegant clothing (Especially Asami, but she's loaded so duh lol). Compare this to Korra's Book 4 formal outfit and the difference is night and day. The loose, flowy sleeves, intentionally exposed shoulders, the more striking colour palette, her necklace, everything feels so much more refined, intentional, and matured, just like her at this point in time. Her Book 1 outfit corresponded with her mindset at the time: awkward and unsure of herself, a kid trying her best to fit into an adult situation with peers far beyond her maturity, while her Book 4 outfit is not only representative of her becoming a more experienced Avatar, but someone who has finally settled into their adult identity and doesn't feel like she's just trying to fake it. She is visibly so much more comfortable, and despite the two outfits being just 4 years apart, you could have easily fooled me and said that Korra was in her 30s in the Book 4 outfit because of how much more mature she looks based on the outfit alone.
label: analysis

text: Many people criticize this scene for destroying past lives, but I hate it because it robs Unalaq of any nuance he had and makes him cartoonishly evil. He's been talking all season about balance and how spirits and humans should live together, that Wan separating Raava and Vaatu was a terrible thing and that he intends to correct that by becoming the new Avatar and bringing true balance to the world. But the first thing he does as the new Avatar is literally destroy the balance again by killing Raava and tipping the scales in Vaatu's favor. How is this better than what Wan did? I'd say it's even worse, because at least Wan only imprisoned Vaatu and didn't destroy him. If Unalaq wanted to restore balance, he should have merged with Raava rather than destroying her, and if this couldn't be done without her consent, he could've killed Korra, since he no longer can convince her, and tried to groom the next reincarnation into helping him.

text: Idk if this is a hot take, but I think Season 1 should've done way more to show the bad sides of Earth, Water and even Airbenders. So something odd I noticed about Season 1 is that, when it talks about some terrible events related to Bending (which is an important theme involving non-benders trying to rise up and encourage ridding the world of bending), there's way more negativity formed in regards to Firebending than the other styles. Mako and Bolin's parents? Killed by Firebenders. Asami's mom? Killed by Firebenders. Amon's fake backstory is specifically centered around being burnt by a Firebender. Now I'm not saying the other styles are shown to be negative, it's just... they're done so way less. In terms of Season 1, the only instance where I can understand hatred being extended to Water and Earth bending is because of Tarrlok abusing his power, and the Earthbending police trying that mass arrest of several innocent civilians. But I don't really count that because those instances only occured after the whole Equalist stuff even began. So I'm just confused why, from the start, the Equalist movement was formed mostly in adversity to Firebenders (they were the reason Asami's dad joined them and ofc Amon's fake story), and yet they're just as willing to harm the other forms of bending, which is very out-of-nowhere, especially for Airbending. Like, I'm not saying the Airbenders should've done something evil, but I'm wondering why an entire crowd would be chanting and cheering for a bunch of pacifists who stay to themselves to lose their bending eternally.
label: analysis

text: imho destroying past lives was a good and logical writing decision and i dont mind it at all. aang heavily leans on his past lives in atla because its been 100 years since the last avatar and there's no one around who can teach him about what the avatar entails. there's even an episode where earth nation soldiers attempt to trigger aang's avatar state by chucking mud at him because no one knew how it worked - enter roku, who explains the mechanics and costs behind the avatar state. but korra has been training to be the avatar her whole life. from the white lotus, katara, and tenzin, she doesnt really need her past lives as much (save for the scene at the end of s1, which i thought was perfect). you can clearly see how much more the world knows about the avatar in tlok, seeing how zaheer knew exactly how to end the avatar cycle through mercury poisoning. more importantly, katara and toph are there to counsel korra through her tough times, in substitute of the avatar wisdom. if you ask me, the whole arc in s4 with korra and toph is way more interesting than korra simply receiving advice from aang himself or any of the past avatars we've seen dozens of times in atla. its also absolutely necessary to remove her past lives as a build-up to "korra alone" by not giving anyone for korra to possibly lean on (just like "zuko alone" in atla). and honestly, the fans love aang so much that reintroducing him constantly in tlok would take the spotlight away from korra and she wouldn't shine as much as her own character. sad as it is, forcing viewers to accept that aang is gone will encourage them to move on and focus on the originality of korra. while it is disappointing that the next avatar won't have access to their past lives, i do like the idea that they will try to get it back continuing the cycle of avatars "fixing" the mistakes of their predecessor.

text: I don’t understand why everyone thinks Ursa failed Azula. The only time we see anything about it is when Azula is in Zuko’s room bragging about how their father was going to murder him and how gleefully she recounted the story was sick, and Ursa put her in her place. You don’t let one of your kids torment the other for fun. As a parent that would be terrifying and heartbreaking all at the same time. Not to mention, he was absolutely going to kill Zuko. And Ursa was not about to let anyone torment him while she was still around. Azula is not okay in the head. Probably never was. People, she needs intense psychological treatment and probably medication forever to even her out to not try and murder people. Not just a tea time with uncle and mommy.
label: analysis

text: I don’t like how everyone suggests avatar get more powerful with reincarnations, there is literally no evidence to suggest this. We never see Roku, Aang or Korra do as big of a bending feat as Kyoshi, nor as big of an airbending feat as yangchen. Roku never says it allows you to be more powerful and do the same amount of feats as the previous avatars. By this logic Wan should be a terrible bender, but he is still more skilled than most avatars. Also it kills all interest from a writing standpoint
label: analysis

text: Sokka’s relationship with his mom is too unexplored. It’s one of Katara’s defining characteristics that she feels anger and guilt that her mom sacrificed herself so that Katara could be the last waterbender of the southern water tribe, but sometimes it does come off that she feels she loved their mother more than Sokka. And that’s okay if she feels truly that way btw, she can have a realistic character flaw, I just wish we had a moment in the show where Sokka discusses their mom either to Suki or Zuko so that it’s clear that her death truly impacted him too
label: analysis

text: Ozai’s pretty one dimensional, but that’s all AtLA really needed. Imo the real villain in AtLA was the Fire Nation as a whole; Ozai was just a symbol. Korra villains were much more complex and that definitely helped LoK tho.

text: Azula logically would've become the strongest firebender of her time. The only people of her era who are widely believed to have been better firebenders than her are Iroh and Ozai. Logically by her 20s to 30s, Azula would've put her father and uncle to shame with her skill.
